entry.title: Time Series Forcasting with TCN
entry.content:
This post introduce multi-variates time-series forecasting using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs).

Multivariates time series

Multivariate time series are catholic in many real world applications, for example, healthcare, financial marketing, IoT. Usually we denote a multivariate time series $\mathbf{X}=\left\{\mathbf{x}_{1}, \mathbf{x}_{2}, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_{T}\right\}$ as a sequence of $T$ observations, the $t-t h$ observation $\mathbf{x}_{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{D}$

Usually, the time-series will in form:

feature 1feature 2feature 3…feature M
$t1$
$t2$
…
$tN$

Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs)

What is TCN?

  • 1D fully-convolutional network(FCN):

    The FCN is widely used in semantic segmentation tasks, 1D FCN have a structure where each hidden layer is the same size as the input layer. Hence, the model is able to handle the sequence-to-sequence tasks.

  • Causal dialated convolutions:

    To prevent from leakage by using future information, causal convolutions were applied, where an output at time $t$ is convolved only with elements from time $t$ and earlier in the previous layer.

    As simple causal convolution is only able to look back at a history with size linear in the depth of the network, when the input is a long sequence, the network have to be quite deep to obtain a receptive field that covers the input. To tackle this challenge, dialated convoluntion was employed, which enable an exponentially large receptive field.

Why using TCN?

  1. Flexible receptive field size and variable length inputs

    The receptive field size of a TCN can be changed in multiple ways:

    • making network deeper by stacking more dilated (causal) convolutional layers

    • using larger dilation factors

    • increasing the filter size.

TCNs thus afford better control of the model’s memory size, and are easy to adapt to different domains.

  1. Parallel computations

    Unlike conventional RNNs, where the predictions for next timesteps must wait for the previous timesteps to complete, convolutions can be done in parallel since the same filter is used in each layer. Therefore, in both training and evaluation, a long input sequence can be processed as a whole in TCN, instead of sequentially as in RNN.

  2. Stable gradients

    Unlike conventional RNNs, the backpropagation path of TCNs different from the time direction of the sequence, thus avoid the problem of exploding/vanishing gradients, which is the major problem of RNNs.

  3. Low memory requirement for training

    As in a TCN the filters are shared across a layer, with the backpropagation path depending only on network depth, the memory usage could significantly reduced, especially in the case of a long input sequence, where LSTMs and GRUs can easily use up a lot of memory to store the partial results for their multiple cell gates.

Implement TCN for time-series forecasting tasks in PyTorch

Data preparation

As TCN is slide over time-axis, we need to prepare our data with a shape of $[N, C, L]$, where $N$ is the batch-size, $C$ is the number of features, $L$ is the sequence length. The time-series now looks:

$t1$$t2$$t3$…$tN$
feature 1
feature 2
…
feature M

Implementation details

Reference: https://github.com/locuslab/TCN 

import torchimport torch.nn as nnfrom torch.nn.utils import weight_normclass Chomp1d(nn.Module):    def __init__(self, chomp_size):        super(Chomp1d, self).__init__()        self.chomp_size = chomp_size    def forward(self, x):        return x[:, :, :-self.chomp_size].contiguous()class TemporalBlock(nn.Module):    def __init__(self, n_inputs, n_outputs, kernel_size, stride, dilation, padding, dropout=0.2):        super(TemporalBlock, self).__init__()        self.conv1 = weight_norm(nn.Conv1d(n_inputs, n_outputs, kernel_size,                                           stride=stride, padding=padding, dilation=dilation))        self.chomp1 = Chomp1d(padding)        self.relu1 = nn.ReLU()        self.dropout1 = nn.Dropout(dropout)        self.conv2 = weight_norm(nn.Conv1d(n_outputs, n_outputs, kernel_size,                                           stride=stride, padding=padding, dilation=dilation))        self.chomp2 = Chomp1d(padding)        self.relu2 = nn.ReLU()        self.dropout2 = nn.Dropout(dropout)        self.net = nn.Sequential(self.conv1, self.chomp1, self.relu1, self.dropout1,                                 self.conv2, self.chomp2, self.relu2, self.dropout2)        self.downsample = nn.Conv1d(n_inputs, n_outputs, 1) if n_inputs != n_outputs else None        self.relu = nn.ReLU()        self.init_weights()    def init_weights(self):        self.conv1.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01)        self.conv2.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01)        if self.downsample is not None:            self.downsample.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01)    def forward(self, x):        out = self.net(x)        res = x if self.downsample is None else self.downsample(x)        return self.relu(out + res)class TemporalConvNet(nn.Module):    def __init__(self, num_inputs, num_channels, kernel_size=2, dropout=0.2):        super(TemporalConvNet, self).__init__()        layers = []        num_levels = len(num_channels)        for i in range(num_levels):            dilation_size = 2 ** i            in_channels = num_inputs if i == 0 else num_channels[i-1]            out_channels = num_channels[i]            layers += [TemporalBlock(in_channels, out_channels, kernel_size, stride=1, dilation=dilation_size,                                     padding=(kernel_size-1) * dilation_size, dropout=dropout)]        self.network = nn.Sequential(*layers)    def forward(self, x):        return self.network(x)

When forwarding, if we want output as the same length as the input, set is_sequence=True, or set is_sequence=False if we only need to predict the last time-step.

import torchfrom torch import nnfrom models.tcn import TemporalConvNetimport torch.nn.functional as Fclass TCNs(nn.Module):    def __init__(self, input_size, output_size, num_channels, kernel_size, dropout, is_sequence):        super(TCNs, self).__init__()        self.tcn = TemporalConvNet(input_size, num_channels, kernel_size, dropout=dropout)        self.linear = nn.Linear(num_channels[-1], output_size)        self.is_sequence = is_sequence    def init_weights(self):        self.linear.weight.data.normal_(0, 0.01)    def forward(self, inputs):        """Inputs have to have dimension (N, C_in, L_in)"""        y1 = self.tcn(inputs)        # if we want output as the same length as the input        if self.is_sequence:            o = self.linear(y1.transpose(1, 2))        # if we only want the output from the last time step        else:            o = self.linear(y1[:, :, -1])        return o

Reference

Bai, Shaojie, J. Zico Kolter, and Vladlen Koltun. “An empirical evaluation of generic convolutional and recurrent networks for sequence modeling.” arXiv preprint arXiv:1803.01271 (2018).
entry.summary: This post introduce multi-variates time-series forecasting using Temporal Convolutional Networks (TCNs).

entry.title: Support Vector Machine
entry.content:
An throughout introduction to Support Vector Machine.

Introduction

Support Vector Machine is a very famous classification algorithm ( can be used in regression challenges as well ) in Machine Learning, which occupied the dominant position of classification algorithms before Deep Learning. Even today, there are still many cases where SVM will be used.

This article will explain SVM in detail, and in the end I will show the MATLAB codes for implementing different SVMs, which can help you to have a deeper understanding on how SVM works.

What is SVM

SVM is a kind of supervised machine learning algorithm, which usually used for two-classes classification.

There are 6 key parts in SVM:

  • Margins
  • Primal Problem
  • Lagrange
  • Dual Problem
  • Soft Margin
  • Kernel

Before we go into details, let’s start with figuring out what exactly SVM is doing.

Let’s consider a scenario, ChewChew needs to apply for a loan from the bank to buy a house. The bank needs to decide whether to approve ChewChew’s application.

Usually, the bank decided by age-income, and the bank had the following historical data:

What should bank do if they want make the decision based on historical data?

We may naturally want to draw a line to divide the historical data into two parts, if ChewChew drop in the upper half part, then approve his application, else rejection.

The line we draw also called Hyperplane

In geometry, a hyperplane is a subspace whose dimension is one less than that of its ambient space. If a space is 3-dimensional then its hyperplanes are the 2-dimensional planes, while if the space is 2-dimensional, its hyperplanes are the 1-dimensional lines.
One Hyperplane can be denoted by $g(\mathbf{x})=\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}+b=0$

This Hyperplane will classify a given data :

If the Hyperplane classify a given data correctll, we have:

Now comes the problem, is there only one Hyperplane?

Obviously the answer is no, since the Hyperplane will directly affect ChewChew’s loan, so the “quality” of the Hyperplane is quite important.

How can we find the optimal Hyperplane?
This is exactly the problem that SVM is solving, in general, SVM provides a systematic way to divide data optimal.

How we define the “Optimal” Hyperplane?
To answer this question, we need to understand what is “Margin”.

Margins

Margin, the gap that separate two classes of data, is the key concept in SVM。

In general, there are three kinds of Margin：

  • Functional Margin
  • Geometric Margin
  • Margin of a training set

we can subdivide the Margin：

  • $\gamma_{i}^{f}$ : Functional margin of an example $\mathbf({x_i, d_i})$
  • $\gamma_{i}^{g}$: Geometric margin of an example $\mathbf({x_i, d_i})$
  • $\gamma^{f}$: Functional margin of a training set
  • $\gamma^{g}$: Geometric margin of a training set
  • $\gamma$: Margin of a training set

Let’s take a look on the mathematic definition of each kind of Margin:

For a given Hyperplane $(\mathbf{w}, b)$：

1. Functional Margin $\gamma_{i}^{f}$ of an example $\mathbf({x_i, d_i})$

2. Geometric margin $\gamma_{i}^{g}$ of an example $\mathbf({x_i, d_i})$

We can prove that the physical meaning of $\gamma_{i}^{g}$ is the Euclidean distance from ${x_i}$ to our Hyperplane, the mathematical proof will attached in reference later, now we focus on meaning of Margin.

According to the mathematical definition，we can found that the relation between $\gamma_{i}^{f}$ and $\gamma_{i}^{g}$：

3. Functional margin $\gamma^{f}$ of a training set $S$
The minimal ${\gamma_{i}^{f}}$ under $S$:

4. Geometric margin $\gamma^{g}$ of a training set $S$
The minimal ${\gamma_{i}^{g}}$ under$S$ :

From the definition we know that one dataset can have different Geometric Margin, which depends on how we choose the Hyperplane.

Thus, we can define Optimal Hyperplane more clearer:

For a given dataset $S$, the Optimal Hyperplane is the one with the maximum $\gamma^{g}$, and this $\gamma^{g}$ is the Margin for dataset $S$:

For example, assume we have a dataset $S$, in order to get Margin for $S$, we need to know all the Geometric margin of $S$ under all possible hyperplane:

Step 1. For Hyperplane 1 $\left(\mathbf{w}_{1}, b_{1}\right)$，calculate $\gamma_{i}^{g}$ for all data points：

Step 2. Find the minimal $\gamma_{i}^{g}$ under Hyperplane 1 ，here we assume it is the $\gamma_{1}^{g}$ for ${x}_{1}$:

Step 3. For Hyperplane 2，Hyperplane 3，…… , Hyperplane N… repeat step 1 and step 2.

Step 4. Find the maximal $\gamma_{\left(\mathrm{w}, b\right)}^{g}$ we calculated in step 3：

Shown as follow:

For now, these is all we need to know about Margin, Margin is one of the core concepts of SVM, once you understand Margin, you could easily understand the rest part of SVM.

Conclusion for Margin:

Margin is used to represent the gap that can separate two-classes data points, we want the gap as wide as possible, in order to achieve this, we calculate the Euclidean distance to each possible Hyperplane for each data point, and use the minimal Euclidean distance as the $\gamma_{i}^{g}$ under this Hyperplane, this is because we want separate two classes as accurate as possible under this Hyperplane.

Then we can get the maximal over all $\gamma_{\left(\mathrm{w}, b\right)}^{g}$ we have calculated, this is because we want our model be able to tolerate noise from test set, and be robust to different data.

There are some mathematical proof for the two “because” I mentioned above, however, it is not recommended that those new to SVM are caught in various incomprehensible mathematical proofs, some proof we just take it as fact.

ps. Those who want study the mathematical proofs about SVM can refer to the book written by Cristianini and Shawe-Taylor…

After we are able to understand Margin，we will think about a question，for a given training set $S$，How could we find out the optimal Hyperplane？

To answer this question, let’s start from Primal Problem …

Primal Problem

The Primal Problem comes from the question:

How to find the margin of $S$

We can recall the relationship:

This relationship show that for a given $\mathbf{w}$, the example $k$ that yields $\gamma_{k}^{f}=\gamma^{f}$ also yields $\gamma^{g}=\gamma_{k}^{g}$, so we can write:

Then, for maximizing $\gamma^{g}$, we can:

Fixing $\gamma^{f}$ + Minimizing $|\mathbf{w}|$

  1. Fixing $\gamma^{f}$:
    Since we can scale a hyperplane by a constant $c$ and keep it invariant, for any hyperplane $(\mathbf{w}, b)$, we can find a constant $c$ such that:

    That is, we fix functional margin of training set to be 1, with this, for any example $\mathbf{x}_{i}$, we have:

  2. Minimizing $|\mathbf{w}|$:
    With the condition above, the problem of minimizing $|\mathbf{w}|$ can be expressed as a $constrained$ $optimization$ $problem$:

    Solving this problem yields the optimal hyperplane $\left(\mathbf{w}_{o}, b_{o}\right)$

Hence, we can express the Primal Problem as:

Given dataset: $S=\left\{\left(\mathbf{x}_{i}, d_{i}\right)\right\}, i=1,2, \ldots, N$

Find: $\mathbf{w}$ and $b$

Minimizing: $f(\mathbf{w})=\frac{1}{2} \mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{w}$

Subject to: $d_{i}\left(\mathbf{w}^{T} \mathbf{x}_{i}+b\right) \geq 1$

To solve the primal problem, we need introduce Lagrange first.

Lagrange

Updating…
entry.summary: An throughout introduction to Support Vector Machine.
